exercises.title: A: Multiple Choice (Travel)
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C, or D).
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C, or D).
exercises.passage: Travelling to new places can (1) .......... a great deal of effort and planning. It also helps to be naturally (2) .......... . However, you need to be (3) .......... of the local customs and traditions wherever you go. When Maria first travelled abroad, the city she visited was (4) .......... of tourists and she felt a little overwhelmed. Her guide told her she should not (5) .......... too much about feeling lost, and that confusion would not (6) .......... her from enjoying the experience. A positive (7) .......... of being in an unfamiliar place is that it can broaden your perspective. Rather than being (8) .......... in knowledge about other cultures, most experienced travellers find that challenges help them grow. A travel expert (9) .......... travellers to treat every difficulty as an opportunity. It is important to (10) .......... the culture of the place you are visiting and what it expects of visitors. If you want to make a (11) .......... out of travel writing, you must learn to embrace the unexpected. Maria's first overseas trip was a complete (12) .......... but she refused to be discouraged. She made a decision to (13) .......... travelling, regardless of how challenging it became. Success does not (14) .......... entirely on a perfect itinerary; flexibility and curiosity are equally important. As Maria discovered, new experiences can (15) .......... to be far more rewarding than you ever expected.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is collocation, so the correct answer is "require".

wrongOptions.A: "need" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "require" fits better.
wrongOptions.need: "need" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "require" fits better.
wrongOptions.B: "use" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "require" fits better.

wrongOptions.spend: "spend" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "require" fits better.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the words before and after the gap together — collocations are heard as a single chunk.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Some word pairs are fixed collocations — the correct option is the one native speakers combine naturally.

explanations: Collocation → can **require** a great deal of effort.
Be mindful **of** local customs (aware and respectful).
Don't **worry** too much about feeling lost.
Would not **deter** her from enjoying the experience.
Not **deficient** in knowledge — challenges help you grow.
Research the culture → important to **research** what it expects.
Depend → Success does not **hinge** entirely on a perfect itinerary.
Turn out → experiences can **prove** to be more rewarding.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Open Cloze (Social media and teenagers)
exercises.title: C: Open Cloze (Social media and teenagers)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage: A new report has revealed a significant rise (1) .......... the use of social media among teenagers. Young people are often regarded (2) .......... being addicted to their smartphones, and many of the teenagers who (3) .......... interviewed said they felt comfortable (4) .......... their online identities. They find it hard to tell (5) .......... truth about how much time they actually spend online. Teachers are largely responsible (6) .......... raising awareness of healthy digital habits. Constant phone use can have a negative effect (7) .......... concentration in the classroom. Many students admitted they stayed (8) .......... very late at night, despite (9) .......... early lessons the following morning. The report, (10) .......... was produced by a leading university, also found that many young people have a limited understanding (11) .......... how social media shapes their self-image. Schools are being urged to (12) .......... action and introduce digital wellness classes. In (13) .......... of these concerns, young people still see technology as having more benefits than drawbacks. Researchers believe (14) .......... are practical steps families can take to encourage healthier habits. (15) .......... the very least, schools should ensure internet safety is part of the curriculum.
explanations: Rise **in** the use of social media.
Non-defining relative → the report, **which** was produced…
Understanding **of** how social media shapes self-image.
Existential → **there** are practical steps families can take.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Future forms (will, going to, present continuous) depend on whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Possessive forms and possessive determiners show who something belongs to.

items.sentenceAfter: last spring and it looked completely different afterwards.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is causative, so the correct answer is "had the flat redecorated".

wrongOptions.A: "had redecorated the flat" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.had redecorated the flat: "had redecorated the flat" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.C: "were redecorated the flat" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

items.sentence: The professor expects all students to hand **F** their assignments before Friday.
items.explanation: Submit work → hand **in** their assignments.

items.sentence: She agreed to take **E** the family business when her father retired.
items.explanation: Take control of → take **over** the family business.

items.sentence: The traffic jam held **H** our journey to the airport by nearly an hour.
items.explanation: Delay → held **up** our journey to the airport.

items.sentence: The doctor advised him to cut **C** fatty foods for the sake of his health.
items.explanation: Reduce consumption → cut **down on** fatty foods.

items.sentence: The police are looking **B** the cause of the fire at the warehouse.
items.explanation: Investigate → looking **into** the cause of the fire.

items.sentence: Sophie had to turn **A** the promotion because of her family commitments.
items.explanation: Reject an offer → turn **down** the promotion.

items.sentence: The explorers decided to set **G** before dawn to beat the midday heat.
items.explanation: Begin a journey → set **off** before dawn.

exercises.title: F: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
Nobody thinks the young athlete has the ability to climb that rocky peak. **capable**
Nobody thinks the young athlete ...... that rocky peak.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ability, so the correct answer is "is capable of climbing".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is capable of climbing", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Sarah, do you know anything about quantum physics? **familiar**
Sarah, ...... quantum physics?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is knowledge of a subject, so the correct answer is "are you familiar with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are you familiar with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
You shouldn't have spent so much money on decorating the entire house. **expense**
You shouldn't have ...... decorating the entire house.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is spend a lot, so the correct answer is "gone to the expense of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "gone to the expense of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
My sister often phones relatives late at night for a chat. **tendency**
My sister ...... relatives late at night for a chat.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is habit, so the correct answer is "has a tendency to phone".

wrongOptions.a tendency to phone: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has a tendency to phone", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Brian seemed completely exhausted when I saw him yesterday. **impression**
Brian ...... he was completely exhausted.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is seemed, so the correct answer is "gave me the impression that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence:
Taking regular exercise will be beneficial for you. **interest**
It is ...... regular exercise.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is beneficial, so the correct answer is "in your interest to take".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "in your interest to take", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I follow what Gary was saying in the meeting, but I'm not fully convinced. **point**
I can ...... in the meeting, but I'm not fully convinced.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is understand an argument, so the correct answer is "see Gary's point".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "see Gary's point", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Security staff will not allow passengers to get on the wrong train. **prevent**
Security staff are going ...... on the wrong train.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is stop from doing, so the correct answer is "to prevent passengers from getting".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "to prevent passengers from getting", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I really need you to try as hard as you can to meet this deadline! **effort**
I really need you to ...... to meet this deadline!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is try hard, so the correct answer is "make a real effort".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "make a real effort", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: G: Error Correction (Extra word)
exercises.instructions: If a line is correct, put a tick (✓). If there is an extra word, write it.
exercises.studentInstruction: If the line is correct, tap OK. If there is an extra word, tap it.

items.sentence: It is often been said that the key to great photography is patience and dedication.
items.explanation: Present simple passive → It **is** often said (remove **been**).

items.sentence: As a photographer myself, I would entirely agree.
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: I was shown that a photograph the other day of an elderly man sitting in a park.
items.explanation: Shown a photograph → shown **a photograph** (remove **that**).

items.sentence: The moment was fleeting and, despite of the fading afternoon light, it was beautiful.
items.explanation: Despite + noun → despite **the** fading light (remove **of**).

items.sentence: The image had a certain [a] timeless quality that I found quite remarkable.
items.explanation: Extra article → a certain timeless quality (remove the extra **a**).

items.sentence: However skilled you are, you simply cannot make out people feel emotion that isn't there.
items.explanation: Make people feel → make people **feel** emotion (remove **out**).

items.sentence: I ask my students to send [to] me their best shots at the end of each week.
items.explanation: Send to someone → send **me** their shots (remove **to**).

items.sentence: If I've never seen anything like it before, and if the image makes me feel [it] genuinely moved,
items.explanation: Make me feel → makes me **feel** something (remove **it**).

items.sentence: I know the photograph has captured something rare and truly meaningful.
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: I love images that capture ordinary life, even though the subject was looks completely unremarkable at first.
items.explanation: Subject looks → the subject **looks** composed (remove **was**).

items.sentence: 'A single ray of light can make up a cold and empty room look quite inviting.'
items.explanation: Make a room look → can **make** a cold room look inviting (remove **up**).

items.sentence: Now that's the real test of any great photographer.
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: Photography is made not just about the technical skill you possess!
items.explanation: Not just about → Photography is **not** just about technical skill (remove **made**).

items.sentence: It is also about patience, perspective, and a willingness to experiment.
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: It takes genuine dedication and also a very sharp natural eye for the world around you.
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

exercises.title: H: Word Formation (University life)
exercises.instructions: Use the word in capitals to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word in capitals to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.passage: There is a popular (1) .......... among university graduates: 'Your student years shape who you become.' Yet no (2) .......... who first hears it truly believes it. University life often brings (3) .......... pressure alongside demanding coursework. When assignments pile up and (4) .......... sets in during long library sessions, it can be hard to appreciate the experience. Yet when it is over, graduates look back fondly on the (5) .......... aspects — the new friendships, the discoveries and the personal freedom. They tend to forget the difficult moments: struggling to pay (6) .......... during a dull lecture, being criticised for poor (7) .......... in a group project, feeling completely (8) .......... when a deadline approaches with no support available, and having completely (9) .......... the requirements for an exam too. But it is entirely (10) .......... to look back with warmth once those challenging times are over.
explanations: No **student** who first hears it believes it.
**Boredom** sets in during long library sessions.
Look back on **exciting** aspects of university life.
Criticised for poor **behaviour** in a group project.
Feel completely **helpless** when a deadline approaches.
Entirely **acceptable** to look back with warmth.
